facts_disclosed: Switched to Yara fertilizer, 95 cedis/bag subsidized, 14 bags, 1,330 total

turns.content: good news today my friend. the government subsidy for fertilizer finally came through
That's great to hear! Fertilizer subsidies can make a real difference for cocoa farmers. What are the details -- which fertilizer and how much of a discount?
Yara brand. the subsidy brings it down to 95 cedis per bag. before I was paying 180 per bag for a different brand so this is almost half price

That's a significant saving -- nearly 50% less per bag. Yara is a reputable fertilizer company with a strong presence in West Africa. Their cocoa-specific formulations are well-regarded.

At 95 cedis per bag, are you planning to buy the same number of bags as before, or adjust your quantity?

this is the beauty of it my friend. because the price is lower I can afford more bags. I am taking 14 bags this season. before I could only manage 12 at the old price. 14 bags at 95 cedis is 1,330 cedis total

Smart move. Let me check that math -- 14 times 95 is indeed 1,330 cedis. Compare that to your old cost: 12 bags at 180 would have been 2,160. So you're spending 830 cedis less and getting 2 extra bags of fertilizer. That's a win on both sides.

More fertilizer coverage across your land should boost yields. Have they given any projections on what to expect?
they say 15% better yield with the Yara formulation plus the extra 2 bags covering more of the farm. when the rain falls on a bargain you carry a bigger bucket my friend

Ha -- that's a perfect way to put it. A 15% yield increase on top of spending less would be excellent. Even if the actual improvement is 10%, you're still ahead because your cost per bag of cocoa produced drops significantly.

Are the other farmers in the cooperative also switching to take advantage of the subsidy?

I am telling them all to register. the subsidy has a deadline, they must apply before January. I have been going farm to farm this week. some of the older men are stubborn. they say they know what works. but 95 cedis versus 180 is not a debate my friend it is mathematics

You're doing the right thing by going farm to farm. Sometimes people need to hear it in person from someone they trust, not just see it on a notice board.

For the stubborn ones, maybe the cost argument alone will work: even if they don't believe in the Yara brand, saving 85 cedis per bag is hard to argue with. That money stays in their pocket for school fees, food, or next season's inputs.

Is the registration process complicated, or fairly straightforward?

you need your farmer ID card and the cooperative registration number. I have the cooperative number so I am helping each farmer fill the form. some cannot read well so I write for them. it is my work as chairman

That's leadership in action -- not just announcing the opportunity but making sure every farmer can actually access it. Filling out forms for those who need help is exactly what a good chairman does.

How many of the 147 families have registered so far?

maybe 90 so far. I have until January to reach the rest. some farms are far and the road is not good in this weather. but I will reach them. 'the ant does not leave its brother behind because the path is long'

90 out of 147 with weeks still to go is good progress. If you can get the majority registered, the whole cooperative benefits from lower input costs and potentially higher yields.

For the remote farms -- could you send word through someone heading that direction? A neighbouring farmer, a market trader, anyone who passes through? Sometimes the message travels faster than the chairman's legs.
good idea. the trotro drivers pass through those areas. they know everyone. I will give them the message

Trotro drivers are the perfect village network -- they see everyone and talk to everyone. Give them the deadline date and the documents needed so farmers can prepare before you visit.

One other thing to think about: once you and the other farmers start using the Yara, keep notes on how the trees respond compared to the old fertilizer. If the results are clearly better, that evidence will make it even easier to convince the holdouts next season.

yes. I will keep record. I have a small notebook where I write the farm things. bags applied, date, which section of the farm. if the yield improves I will have the proof. the old men respect proof more than promises

That notebook is worth its weight in gold. Actual yield data from your own farm is more convincing than anything the government or the fertilizer company can say. If you can show "I used Yara on section A and got X more bags than last year," the stubborn ones will come around.

Keep the records simple: section, date applied, bags used, and then harvest weight later. That's all you need.
I will do it. now I must go my friend. more forms to fill before dark. this subsidy is a blessing but the paperwork is its own harvest. hah. medaase
Ha -- the paperwork harvest! At least this one pays well. Good luck getting the rest registered before the deadline. 1,330 cedis for 14 bags is a deal worth the trouble. Take care.
